mnemonics.title: TM Components Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Tape Head State Reg - Accept Reject Transition (T-H-S-A-R-T) - Think: 'The Head Sits Around Reading Transitions'

mnemonics.title: Chomsky Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Regular < Context-Free < Context-Sensitive < Recursively Enumerable (RE) - Think: 'Regular Cats Sit Eat' (R-C-S-E)

mnemonics.title: Decidable vs Recognizable
mnemonics.content: Decidable: Always HALT (Yes/No). Recognizable: HALT on Yes, LOOP on No. Think: 'Decidable = Done & Decided'

mnemonics.title: Church-Turing Thesis
mnemonics.content: Church = Computable by lambda calculus, Turing = Computable by TM. Both define the same class of functions - Think: 'Church & Turing Agreed on Compute'

acronyms.usage: Abstract computational model that defines what is computable
TM with exactly one transition per state-symbol pair
TM with multiple possible transitions per state-symbol pair
TM that can simulate any other TM given its description
Restricted TM with tape limited to input length; recognizes CSL
Language class recognized by Turing Machines (Turing-recognizable)
Undecidable problem used to prove undecidability of other problems

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a long conveyor belt (tape) with boxes (cells). The read-write head is like a forklift that can read, replace items, and move left or right along the belt.
Think of it as a lookup table: Given (State, Symbol) → tell me (New State, New Symbol, Direction). Like a function machine with three outputs.
Think of a snapshot: State is your location on a map, tape content is the scenery, head position is where you're standing. All three tell you exactly what's happening.
Two special rooms (states) in a building. If you ever enter the Accept Room → success. If you enter Reject Room → failure. Otherwise, you might keep wandering forever.

analogies.analogy: Turing Machine is like a computer with an infinitely long paper tape as memory - modern computers use RAM, but the fundamental capability is the same.
Like asking 'Can you write a program to predict if any other program will crash?' - clearly impossible as you'd have to run the program to know.
Like an interpreter/compiler - it takes code (TM description) as input and executes it, just as a CPU executes machine code.
Like having magic intuition - at each step, you can guess the right path to solution. NTM is 'all-powerful guesser' but DTM can simulate it by trying all paths.
